one_line_takeaway: A survey/taxonomy paper (no original experiments) arguing that embodied-AI security failures often stem from embodiment-induced system-level mismatches -- semantic-physical gaps, state-dependent consequences, cross-layer error propagation, non-compositional safety -- that neither LLM-security nor classical CPS-security framings capture alone.
key_finding: The paper's central claim (not an experimental result but an argued thesis) is that embodied AI security failures frequently cannot be attributed to either an LLM vulnerability or a classical CPS flaw in isolation, but instead emerge from structural mismatches introduced by embodiment itself -- e.g., a plan that is semantically and logically correct can still be physically unsafe because language-level reasoning abstracts away geometry, friction, contact dynamics, and state uncertainty that determine physical safety. The paper supports this thesis by aggregating third-party evidence (cited, not produced by the authors) rather than by original measurement.
core_contribution: A 26-page survey/position paper (Ma, Guo, Lv, Xu, Dai, Zhang, Yang, Zhang; Shandong University) arguing that neither of the two dominant framings of embodied-AI security -- (1) 'vulnerable brain' LLM-centric threats (prompt injection, jailbreaking) or (2) 'fragile body' classical Cyber-Physical System (CPS) threat models (sensor/control/actuator/timing attacks) -- is individually sufficient to explain observed failures in embodied AI systems (robots, autonomous vehicles, LLM-driven agents). The paper's own contribution is threefold: (a) a taxonomy that systematizes LLM-centric embodied vulnerabilities (semantic/intent-integrity attacks, poisoning/backdoor attacks, multi-modal adversarial attacks) and classical CPS threat categories (Tables 1-7, 10-11), each mapped to violated system-level assumptions; (b) four synthesized 'root cause' insights that the authors argue are embodiment-specific and irreducible to either LLM or CPS security alone: (i) semantic correctness does not imply physical safety, (ii) identical actions can yield drastically different outcomes across physical states (nonlinear state-action mapping), (iii) errors propagate and amplify across perception-decision-action loops, and (iv) safety is not compositional across time or system layers; and (c) an enumeration of open challenges (bias inheritance, hallucination, multi-robot coordination, human-factor risks, impossibility of static filters, large attack surfaces, irreversibility, liability/certification standards) intended to motivate future system-level (rather than component-level) security research. The paper reports no new experiments, attacks, defenses, datasets, or benchmarks of its own -- every concrete quantitative claim in the text is attributed to a cited prior work.
threat_model: The paper does not propose or evaluate a single threat model of its own; it synthesizes a taxonomy of threat models drawn from the literature it surveys, organized along two axes it argues are individually incomplete: (1) LLM-centric threats to the decision/reasoning layer -- semantic/intent-integrity attacks (e.g., jailbreaks that produce hardware-executable but unsafe plans, such as the cited POEX attack and Harmful-RLbench benchmark), poisoning/backdoor attacks on training or in-context data, and multi-modal adversarial perturbations against vision-language-action (VLA) models; and (2) classical CPS threats to the perception/actuation layers -- sensor spoofing, control-loop destabilization, actuator manipulation, and timing attacks. The paper's own argued addition to this space is a class of 'embodiment-induced system-level mismatches' that sit outside both categories: failures caused by the interaction of semantically valid high-level reasoning with physical dynamics, state-dependent consequences, cross-layer error propagation, and non-compositional safety constraints, rather than by any single component being compromised by an attacker.
limitations: As a survey/position paper the work has no held-out evaluation of its own claims: the four 'insights' are argued qualitatively and illustrated with selectively cited findings rather than tested against a unified benchmark or ablation. The taxonomy tables classify existing attacks along axes (lifecycle stage, risk level) that are the authors' own construction and not independently validated. Because coverage depends on the surveyed literature, the taxonomy may omit recent or non-English-language work, and the four insights, while intuitively motivated, are not falsifiable claims in the paper's own framing -- there is no proposed metric or experiment by which a reader could confirm or reject that a given failure is 'embodiment-induced' versus attributable to an LLM or CPS flaw. The paper also does not evaluate any defense or mitigation it proposes; Section 7's 'open challenges' are framed as future research directions, not solutions.

quantitative_results: All numeric/empirical claims in the text are attributed to third-party cited works, not produced by this paper's own experiments, e.g.: GPT-4 and Claude 'degrade substantially on embodied safety benchmarks compared to text-only tasks' (attributed to Liu et al. [68]); 'grounding accuracy drops sharply under partial occlusion in language-guided grasping' (attributed to Jiang et al. [45]); LIBERO benchmark 'success rates drop on novel objects even when tasks' otherwise succeed (attributed to LIBERO [63], a cited external benchmark).
